does: This paper proposes Temporal Sparse Autoencoders (T-SAEs), an extension of dictionary learning methods that incorporates temporal structure through a contrastive loss designed to encourage consistent activations of features across adjacent tokens in language sequences. The key contribution is demonstrating that by leveraging the sequential nature of text—where semantic content typically evolves smoothly—SAEs can better disentangle semantic from syntactic features in a self-supervised manner. The method is evaluated across multiple datasets and models, showing that T-SAEs recover smoother and more coherent semantic concepts while maintaining reconstruction quality, and remarkably, they develop clear semantic structure without explicit semantic supervision during training.
connects: This work builds directly on recent dictionary learning approaches like Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) that aim to interpret neural network representations by discovering human-intelligible features. It responds to a known limitation of standard SAEs: they typically extract token-specific, noisy, or highly localized concepts that fail to capture meaningful semantic structure. The paper connects to broader interpretability research seeking to make model internals transparent and to understanding how neural networks process language. It also relates to work on implicit linguistic structure in neural representations and draws on contrastive learning principles that have proven effective in self-supervised learning contexts for discovering coherent structure without labeled data.
enables: This work opens several future directions in mechanistic interpretability and feature analysis. By demonstrating that temporal structure can improve feature disentanglement, it suggests that other inductive biases reflecting language properties could further enhance interpretability methods. The cleaner semantic representations could enable better probing of what linguistic and conceptual information models encode at different layers and positions. The approach could facilitate developing more interpretable models by incorporating temporal coherence into training objectives. Additionally, the self-supervised emergence of semantic structure suggests potential applications to model alignment, editing, and understanding how models develop semantic understanding during training. These stronger interpretability tools could support safety research and auditing of language models.
